questions.text: Which 2 intraoperative adjustments improve visualization and maneuverability during pelvic laparoscopy in obese patients? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: B) Elevation of insufflation pressure to 18 mmHg
D) Operating with the table in full flexion
E) Resection of preperitoneal fat at camera port

questions.explanation: Higher insufflation pressure can increase working space; extra-long instruments improve reach. Reverse Trendelenburg/table flexion are not standard for pelvic access; resecting preperitoneal fat is not a routine maneuver for visualization.
questions.submodule: Module 6.7 – Port Placement with Extremes of BMI

questions.text: Which of the following are incorrect statements about port placement in patients with prior laparotomy? Select all that are incorrect.

answerOptions.text: A) Entry should be made through or near previous scars to reduce new fascial defects
B) Palmer’s point is contraindicated in patients with prior splenic surgery
C) Bowel adhesions to midline scars are common after laparotomy
D) Left upper quadrant access is often used to reduce the risk of encountering bowel adhesions in the lower abdomen
E) Entry under direct visualization minimizes risk of injury in reoperative fields

questions.explanation: Avoid prior scars for initial entry because adhesions beneath scars are common. The other statements are accurate: LUQ access (Palmer’s Point) is helpful unless prior splenic surgery; adhesions to midline scars are common; and direct visualization reduces risk.
questions.submodule: Module 6.8 – Port Placement with Prior Laparotomy

questions.text: Which of the following strategies help reduce complications associated with power morcellation?

answerOptions.text: A) Confirming benign pathology prior to morcellation
B) Avoiding morcellation in postmenopausal women
D) Preoperative endometrial biopsy for all patients
E) Delaying morcellation until histology returns

questions.explanation: Postmenopausal status raises risk of occult malignancy—avoid morcellation. Contained morcellation reduces intraperitoneal dissemination risk. Definitive pathology is not available until after removal; blanket biopsy or waiting for histology is not practical.
questions.submodule: Module 6.9 – Laparoscopic Tissue Extraction

questions.text: Which 3 suture characteristics make them ideal for laparoscopic suturing of the vaginal cuff? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: E) Ability to maintain tensile strength >7 days

questions.explanation: Cuff closure benefits from sutures with low memory (handling), adequate early tensile strength, and delayed absorbability. Low—not high—tissue reactivity is desirable. Filament choice varies by preference.
questions.submodule: Module 6.10 – Needle and Suture Selection

questions.text: Which 2 surgical practices are most consistently associated with lower adhesion formation in laparoscopy? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Copious peritoneal irrigation with saline
B) Use of harmonic scalpel instead of monopolar
D) Routine use of barrier gels
E) Placement of multiple suction drains

questions.explanation: Gentle handling preserves mesothelium; barrier agents have evidence for reducing adhesions. Energy choice and irrigation are less consistent; drains don’t prevent adhesions.
questions.submodule: Module 6.11 – Risk for Adhesions Following Laparoscopy

questions.text: Which of the following clinical scenarios would justify proactive adhesion prevention strategies during laparoscopy? Select best 3 answers.

answerOptions.text: C) Diagnostic laparoscopy in nulliparous patient
D) Laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy for dermoid
E) Prior history of adhesion-related bowel obstruction

questions.explanation: High-risk scenarios include fertility-impacting procedures (myomectomy), bowel surgery, and patients with prior obstruction.
questions.submodule: Module 6.11 – Risk for Adhesions Following Laparoscopy

questions.text: In obese patients, what is the preferred angle of Veress needle insertion at the umbilicus?
questions.explanation: A perpendicular (90°) approach reduces lateral tracking through a thick abdominal wall.
questions.submodule: Module 6.7 – Port Placement with Extremes of BMI

questions.text: What anatomical change occurs in obese patients that most significantly affects laparoscopic entry technique?

answerOptions.text: A) Increased volume of peritoneal fluid
B) Caudal displacement of the umbilicus
C) Decreased distance between skin and bowel
D) Increased sensitivity to insufflation pressures
E) Lateral shift of abdominal vessels

questions.explanation: The umbilicus migrates caudally with higher BMI, altering safe angles and landmarks.
questions.submodule: Module 6.7 – Port Placement with Extremes of BMI

questions.text: Which of the following is the best reason to place accessory trocars perpendicular to the contour of the insufflated abdomen?

answerOptions.text: C) To reduce torque on fascia and defect enlargement
D) To facilitate easier suture passage

questions.explanation: Perpendicular insertion minimizes instrument shaft length within the wall, reducing torque and fascial stretch.
questions.submodule: Module 6.7 – Port Placement with Extremes of BMI

questions.text: What is the preferred site of laparoscopic entry in a patient with prior midline laparotomy and suspected umbilical adhesions?
questions.explanation: Palmer’s Point avoids likely periumbilical adhesions after midline laparotomy.
questions.submodule: Module 6.8 – Port Placement with Prior Laparotomy

questions.text: Why is stomach decompression required before left upper quadrant entry?

answerOptions.text: A) To reduce risk of bowel injury
D) To reduce the risk of gastric injury

questions.explanation: NG/OG decompression minimizes risk of perforating a distended stomach during LUQ entry.
questions.submodule: Module 6.8 – Port Placement with Prior Laparotomy

questions.text: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to posterior fornix Veress needle placement?
questions.explanation: An obliterated cul-de-sac or significant adhesions make posterior fornix entry unsafe.
questions.submodule: Module 6.8 – Port Placement with Prior Laparotomy

questions.text: Which complication is most specifically linked to morcellation of an occult uterine malignancy?
questions.explanation: Dissemination during morcellation can upstage an occult malignancy (e.g., LMS).
questions.submodule: Module 6.9 – Laparoscopic Tissue Extraction

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a known risk associated with morcellation?
questions.explanation: Morcellation generally avoids laparotomy; other listed issues are recognized risks.
questions.submodule: Module 6.9 – Laparoscopic Tissue Extraction

questions.text: Which is the most effective strategy to minimize risk of tissue dissemination during power morcellation?

answerOptions.text: A) Use of thermal ablation prior to fragmentation

questions.explanation: Performing morcellation within a containment system reduces peritoneal dissemination.
questions.submodule: Module 6.9 – Laparoscopic Tissue Extraction

questions.text: Which suture is most appropriate for vaginal cuff closure after total laparoscopic hysterectomy?
questions.explanation: Absorbable synthetic (e.g., polyglactin) maintains strength through early healing.
questions.submodule: Module 6.10 – Needle and Suture Selection

questions.text: What is the main advantage of barbed sutures in laparoscopic surgery?
questions.explanation: Barbs anchor tissue, obviating knots and improving efficiency in laparoscopy.
questions.submodule: Module 6.10 – Needle and Suture Selection

questions.text: Which needle type is best suited for closure of the vaginal cuff?
questions.explanation: Tapered needles pass through soft tissue with less trauma than cutting variants.
questions.submodule: Module 6.10 – Needle and Suture Selection

questions.text: Which of the following is the LEAST effective strategy for adhesion prevention?
questions.explanation: Excess energy use promotes tissue injury and adhesions.
questions.submodule: Module 6.11 – Risk for Adhesions Following Laparoscopy

questions.text: Adhesions form after which of the following biological processes?

answerOptions.text: B) Fibroblast migration and collagen deposition

questions.explanation: Post-injury fibrosis (fibroblast activity and collagen deposition) consolidates adhesions.
questions.submodule: Module 6.11 – Risk for Adhesions Following Laparoscopy

questions.text: Which patient is at greatest risk for adhesion-related complications after laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) Patient with stage I endometriosis
D) Patient with nulliparity and fibroids

questions.explanation: Chronic inflammatory bowel disease predisposes to adhesions and related complications.
questions.submodule: Module 6.11 – Risk for Adhesions Following Laparoscopy
